coredata.dc:title: Electro-spinning of cellulose acetate nanofibers/Fe/carbon dot as photoluminescence sensor for mercury (II) and lead (II) ions
coredata.dc:creator: Ahmadian-Fard-Fini, Shahla
Ghanbari, Davood
Amiri, Omid
Salavati-Niasari, Masoud
coredata.dc:description: The aim of this work is synthesis of magnetic and photoluminescence (PL) nano-fibers for detection of mercury (II) and lead (II) ions that are toxic and their detection are necessary. Prepared nanoparticles are controllable by external magnetic field, so their threat is much lesser than non-magnetic material. Firstly, iron nanoparticles were achieved with ball milling procedure and cellulose acetate (CA) nano-fibers were prepared by electro spinning procedure. Photoluminescence carbon dot were synthesized with citric acid (or lemon extract as green precursor). Fe nanoparticles were added to polymeric fibers and eventually, carbon dots were coated on the fibers by hydrothermal method. Evidence for carbon dot existence on the nanofibers is photo luminescence (blue color) under ultra-violet irradiation; results show reduction in PL intensity by enhancing of toxic heavy metal ions. A novel magnetic nano-fibers as a rapid test sensor for detecting of mercury(II) and lead(II) is presented.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Nanostructures
Nano-fiber
Magnetic
Photoluminescence
Iron

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of nanofiber/Fe/carbon quantum dot nanocomposite

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of (a) ball milling of Fe nanoparticles (b) electro-spinning of cellules acetate/Fe(c) hydrothermal synthesis of CDs (d) sonochemical/hydrothermal for preparation of nanofiber/Fe/CDs nanocomposite.
XRD pattern of (a) Fe nanoparticles (b) Fiber/Fe/Carbon dots nanocomposite.

(A) Optical microscopy images of (a) Fiber/Carbon dots (b) Fiber/Fe/Carbon dots under visible (left) and UV light (right) (B) Tyndall effect of carbon dots in water with three (a) violet (b) green and (c) red lasers (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).
SEM images of (a,b) carbon dots (c,d) Fe nanoparticles.
SEM images of (a,b,c) pure cellulose acetate nano fibers (c,d,e) nanofiber/Fe (90:10) nanocomposites.
SEM images of (a,b,c) fiber/Fe (70:30%) nanocomposites (d,e,f) fiber/Fe/CDs (80:10:10%) nanocomposites.
(A) (a,b) TEM images of fiber/Fe/CDs nanocomposites, (B) EDX analysis of (a) pure fiber (b) nanofiber/Fe/CDs nanocomposite.
FT-IR spectra of (a) cellulose acetate/iron nanofibers (b) CA/Fe/CDs nanofibers.
AGFM curve of (a) Fe nanoparticles (b) Fiber/Fe/carbon dot nanocomposite.
Quenching of PL intensity (a) pure carbon dot (b) by addition of Pb(II) (c) Hg(II) ions (d) UV–vis spectrum of the carbon dot nanocomposite.

head.ce:title: Electro-spinning of cellulose acetate nanofibers/Fe/carbon dot as photoluminescence sensor for mercury (II) and lead (II) ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Nano Science and Nano Technology, University of Kashan, P.O. Box 87317-51167, Kashan, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Nano Science and Nano Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: 1Institute of Nano Science and Nano Technology, University of Kashan, Kashan, P.O. Box 87317-51167, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Science, Arak University of Technology, Arak, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: 2Department of Science, Arak University of Technology, Arak, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry Department, College of Science, University of Raparin, Rania, Kurdistan Region, Iraq
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: 3Chemistry Department, College of Science, University of Raparin, Rania, Kurdistan Region, Iraq

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The aim of this work is synthesis of magnetic and photoluminescence (PL) nano-fibers for detection of mercury (II) and lead (II) ions that are toxic and their detection are necessary. Prepared nanoparticles are controllable by external magnetic field, so their threat is much lesser than non-magnetic material. Firstly, iron nanoparticles were achieved with ball milling procedure and cellulose acetate (CA) nano-fibers were prepared by electro spinning procedure. Photoluminescence carbon dot were synthesized with citric acid (or lemon extract as green precursor). Fe nanoparticles were added to polymeric fibers and eventually, carbon dots were coated on the fibers by hydrothermal method. Evidence for carbon dot existence on the nanofibers is photo luminescence (blue color) under ultra-violet irradiation; results show reduction in PL intensity by enhancing of toxic heavy metal ions. A novel magnetic nano-fibers as a rapid test sensor for detecting of mercury(II) and lead(II) is presented.

ce:section.ce:para: Electro-spinning (ES) is an electrostatic technical skill includes charging the polymer surface and causes liquid emission from a spinneret between polymer and collector. Voltage, distance, shape and speed of collector affect on the nanofibers, in this process the jet grows longer and thinner until it dries on the collector (Ghanbari, Salavati-Niasari, Beshkar, & Amiri, 2015; Ghanbari, Salavati-Niasari, Khaghani, & Beshkar, 2016). Concentration of polymer should be high enough leads to polymer entanglement yet not so high that the viscosity precludes polymer ejection (Kavousi, Goodarzi, Ghanbari, & Hedayati, 2019). This method can be used in tissue scaffolds, smart clothing, filtration, electrode, pharmaceutical, sensors and environmental engineering. By ES adjustable porous, malleable, size and shape controllable nano-fibers, pure polymeric, ceramic and nanocomposites can be achieved. Viscosity should be optimum, when it is low, jet collapse to droplet before drying, in this condition instead of nano-fibers usually nanospheres (micro-structure particles) are obtained before evaporation of solvent (Ahmadian-Fard-Fini, Salavati-Niasari, & Ghanbari, 2018; Ahmadian-Fard-Fini, Salavati-Niasari, & Ghanbari, 2019).

Carbon dots (CDs) as a new spherical fragment of graphene are bio-compatible in comparison to first generation of quantum dots that usually were prepared from toxic materials (Barman & Patra, 2018; Bera, Qian, Tseng, & Fiberay, 2010; Das, Bandyopadhyay, & Pramanik, 2018; Lin, Luo, Tsai, Wang, & Chen, 2018; Tian, Tang, Teng, & Lau, 2018; Wang et al., 2016; 2017; Wang, Li et al., 2018; Wang, Li, Li et al., 2018; Wang, Wu et al., 2018). C-dots have down and up-converted photo-luminescence (PL), multi-photon excitation, wide range light emitting, functionalization, appropriate dispersion, stability and suitable electron transfers. Its photoluminescence may from excitons, quantum-confinement, aromatic structures and imperfections (Abbas, Mariana, & Phan, 2018; Abhilash, 2010; Baker & Baker, 2010; Kiani, Nabiyouni, Masoumi, & Ghanbari, 2019; Wang, Li et al., 2018; Wang, Li, Li et al., 2018; Wang, Li, Li et al., 2018; Wang, Wu et al., 2018). One of the main applications of fluorescent carbon dots is cell imaging (biomedical) while most of conventional quantum dots are toxic for human being (Balcht & Smith, 1994; Sharma, Umar, Sood, Mehta, & Kansal, 2018; Son, Cohen, & Louie, 2006; Wang, Li et al., 2018; Wang, Li, Li et al., 2018; Wang, Wu et al., 2018; Yu et al., 2018; Zhu et al., 2009). In this work, magnetic and fluorescent nanofiber/Fe/CDs were prepared with applying of ball milling, electro-spinning, sonochemical and hydrothermal methods. Photoluminescence carbon dot were synthesized with citric acid and ethylene diamine by hydrothermal method. Fe nanoparticles were added to polymeric fibers and carbon dots were coated on the fibers by sonochemical-assisted hydrothermal method. Magnetic property is because of inner Fe nanoparticles and photo-luminescence is due to surface adsorbed carbon dots. Results show reduction in PL intensity by enhancing of toxic heavy metal ions. A novel magnetic nano-fibers as an immediate examination sensor for detecting of mercury(II) and lead(II) is presented. Orbitals (d) of lead(II) and mercury (II) can take electron from excited CDs. Interaction of Hg2+ (or Pb2+) and CDs leads to complex formation, therefore electron transfer between electron donor and acceptor was happened, as a result fluorescence of the carbon dot was reduced. (Wang et al., 2016, 2017; Wang, Li et al., 2018; Wang, Li, Li et al., 2018; Wang, Wu et al., 2018)

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of nanofiber/Fe/carbon quantum dot nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: For preparation of carbon dots 0.01 mol of ethylene diamine and 0.01 mol of citric acid (CA or 1 ml of lemon juice) were added to the water. Then the dispersion was transferred into Teflon lined stainless autoclave at 180 °C for 24 h. The final product was soluble in water. Iron nanoparticles were prepared from ball milling procedures, 3000 rpm, six hours in N2 surrounding (Jafari-Fesharaki & Ghanbari, 2017). For preparation of polymeric nanocomposite, 2 g of cellulose acetate was added to 5 ml of acetone-dimethylacetamide (1:1) and the solution was stirred for 2 h, then Fe nanoparticles (0.1 g) were added to 5 ml of acetone under ultrasound irradiation (150 W, 30 min). The dispersion of Fe nanoparticles was added to the polymer solution, firstly the solution was irradiated under sono-chemical waves (150 W, 60 min), then it was stirred for 5 h, and was spun at room temperature (voltage: 13 KV), space in the middle of injectors and collector was about 20 cm. Finally, for preparation of nanofiber/Fe/carbon quantum dot nanocomposite following procedure was applied. 0.5 g of cellulose acetate/Fe nanofibers were immersed in 50 ml of solvent, then 0.1 g of carbon dot was dispersed to the solution applying ultrasonic irradiation (100 W, 60 min), and the dispersion was putted into Teflon coated stainless steel autoclave. It was maintained at 200 °C for 24 h, after reaction the solution was cooled and centrifuged. The final nanocomposite material was washed by distilled water and ethanol.

Schematic of synthesized fiber/Fe/carbon dot (a) ball milling of iron nanoparticles (b) electro-spinning of cellulose acetate-iron nanofibers (c) hydrothermal synthesis of carbon dot (d) sono-chemical procedure for coating of carbon dots on the surface of the nanofibers are depicted in Fig. 1
a-d respectively.

Fig. 2
a shows XRD analysis of iron nanoparticles obtained by ball milling that shows accordance to literature with no major impurity. Pure cubic phase of iron (JCPDS No. 01-087-0721, space group is Im-3 m, number is 229) can be observed in this pattern (without oxidation like Fe2O3 and Fe3O4). Fig. 2 b illustrates XRD pattern of nanofiber/Fe/carbon dot product, presence of Fe (JCPDS: 01-087-0721) is confirmed, presence of crystalline iron beside amorphous cellules acetate (noisy plates) simultaneously approved.

For photoluminescence observation of fiber/CDs samples were placed under optical microscopy and ultraviolet irradiation. Nanofibers are brown under usual light; while, they emit blue under UV irradiation (Fig. 3
(A)). Fig. 3 (B) shows Tyndall effect of carbon dots in water with three various wavelengths by violet, green and red lasers. Light is scattering by nanoparticles in a colloid or in a suitable suspension. For better comparison, distilled water was also put besides of the carbon dot solutions. In Tyndall effect, wavelengths that are longer show greater transmission despite the wavelengths that are shorter show more reflection.

Fig. 4
a and b depict SEM image of C-dots obtained from hydrothermal reaction that confirm preparation of nanostructures, average diameter size is about 30 nm. After hydrothermal carbon structures were dispersed in the solvent by ultrasonic waves, bubbles are created in the water and by collapsing of bubbles some micro-jet are produced that are suitable for breaking of bonds and decrease in agglomeration. At the first step, we wanted to produce CD with these precursors just by applying ultrasonic irradiation but carbon dots did not show suitable luminescence. With these precursors, the best experimental method for preparation of CDs was hydrothermal. For better dispersion ultra-sound waves was applied after hydrothermal. In a sealed reactor by increasing of temperature in a constant volume (according to Amonton’s law) higher pressure is produced and as a result, CDs were successfully prepared at high pressure. Results approve the mediocre particle size is around 15 nm.

SEM images of Fe nanoparticles that were prepared by ball milling method are shown in Fig. 4 c,d. Iron nanoparticles tend to agglomerate to each other because of magnetic property, also ball milling is not very effective for preparation of very small nanoparticles. As we know, it is a top-down method and on the other hand very appropriate for preparation of pure iron without iron oxide impurities. We examined other sono-chemical, microwave and sol-gel methods for preparation of pure Fe (without oxidation: Fe2O3 and Fe3O4 product). While all mentioned chemical procedures show preparation of smaller nanoparticles but they could not prepare pure iron nanoparticles easily. Chemical methods lead to preparation of iron oxide production by interaction with dissolved oxygen in solvents if the operator doesn’t use inert atmosphere.

Fig. 5
a,b,c depicts different magnification SEM of cellulose acetate nanofiber synthesized under electro-spinning (13 kV, 20 cm, 1 h) that show synthesis of mono-disperse nanofibers (the diameter of fibers is around 150 nm). Weight of molecule, viscosity, dielectric constant, surface charge and tension affect the nanofiber size, so we used 13 kV and 20 cm distance for preparation of cellulose acetate nanofibers.

Fig. 5 d,e,f show various magnification SEM images of nano-fiber/Fe 90%:10% applying mechanical and sono-chemical methods, presence of Fe nanoparticles on the fibers was confirmed by these images beside X-ray diffraction pattern, Energy-dispersive X-ray (EDS) spectroscopy and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) approve presence of iron in the polymeric matrix.

Fig. 6
a,b,c depict SEM (from various sites) of CA nanofiber/Fe 70%:30% that also were synthesized at 13KV, 20 cm for 1 h, as the SEM confirms size of the nanostructures are less than 100 nm. Images obviously show nanoparticles on the surface of the polymers. For better dispersion of magnetic iron nanoparticles in nano-fibers we used ultrasonic waves. Beside dispersion application, ultrasonic waves also can decrease size of the particle by micro-jets. According to literature by collapsing of the air bubbles in the water a lot of micro jet are created, these jet produce hot spots in the solvent. These spots are suitable reactor for breaking of bonds and preparation of smaller clusters and nanoparticles. Fig. 6 d,e,f depict SEM of fiber/Fe/Carbon dot 80%:10%:10% that approve presence of magnetic iron and photoluminescence carbon dot on the nano fibers. Our experimental reason for existence evidence of CDs on the nanofibers is photo luminescence nanofibers (blue color) under ultra-violet irradiation while without carbon dot (pure cellulose acetate or cellulose acetate/Fe) we never have photo luminescence under UV–vis excitation.

As we know SEM is not very appropriate for polymeric nanocomposite characterization so for precise investigation transmission electron microscopy was used. Fig. 7
(A) illustrate transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of nanofiber/Fe that confirm adjusted fiber matrix with nano-fillers were observed. TEM images of the achieved product approve fiber nanostructures are similar in the size (diameter around 90 nm) and presence of the nano-additives (diameters of nanofillers: less than 60 nm) in the polymeric matrix is confirmed. Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy of the cellulose acetate nanofibers and Fiber/Fe/CDs nanocomposite are shown in Fig. 7 B(a) and B(b). As the spectrum shows, the Kα peaks of oxygen, carbon and nitrogen elements appear clearly. Kα, Lα and Kβ peaks of Fe element are also confirmed in Fig. 7 B(b). As the EDX spectrum shows, there are no other impurity related to the precursors.

Fig. 8 a depicts Fourrier transform infra-red of the cellulose acetate/iron nanofiber, broad peak at 2944 and 3488 cm−1 are corresponding to carbon-hydrogen and oxygen-hydrogen bonds respectively. Peak at 1050 and 1750 cm−1 are related to C–O and CO bonds of citric acid. Fig. 8b show spectrum of the nanofiber/Fe/CDs nanocomposite. Peaks at 901, 1054, 1158, 1240 cm−1 are for carbonyl group. Absorption at 2936 cm−1 is related to C–H bonds, peak at 1747 cm−1 is related to carboxyl bonds, peak about 604 cm−1 are for small Fe-O bonds.

Alternating gradient force magnetometer (AGFM) instrument was applied for studying of magnetic properties. AGFM of iron nanoparticles and fiber/Fe/carbon nanocomposite are shown in Fig. 9
a and b respectively. The magnetic product can be attracted by an external magnet and eliminate probable risk of nano dimensions. Super paramagnetic product with a saturation magnetization about 95 emu/g and coercivity around 10 Oe (Oe) was approved. Fiber/Fe/carbon nanocomposite illustrates ferromagnetic behavior which its Ms is 22 emu/g and its coercivity approximately is 120 Oe.

Magnetic analysis of iron nanoparticles that were covered by nanofibers was studied. Composition of nanoparticles and nanofibers enhanced the coercivity from 10 Oe to 120 Oe in comparison to pure iron. It seems magnetic domains are stick together by polymeric nano fibers and therefore stronger magnetic field is needed.

Photo luminescence (PL) of untreated carbon dots prepared at 200 °C and reacted with Pb(II) and Hg(II) are shown in Fig. 10
a-c respectively. Intensive PL peak for pure product at wavelength 580 nm was achieved (excitation: 450 nm). Outcomes show by applying hydrothermal and sono-chemical suitable PL is obtained. Decreasing in PL by enhancing in lead (II) and mercury(II) ions was observed. These nanofiber composites introduce appropriate sensor for unhealthy heavy metal ions detection. Orbital (d) of lead(II) and mercury (II) can take electron from CDs that have excitation. By connection of Pb(II) and Hg(II) ions, the fluorescence of the CDs was reduced because of electron transfer in complex formation (interaction of Hg(II) and carbon dots). Hazardous ions can be detected and measured by fluorescence analyze (Ahmadian-Fard-Fini et al., 2018, 2019). Ultraviolet-visible absorption spectrum of carbon dots is depicted in Fig. 10 d. The C-dots which are achieved by citric acid-ED illustrates absorption bands around 380 nm (π-π* transition). Non-zero band gap is obtained because of CDs quantum confinement, so it has UV absorption spectrum and show PL emission.

Carbon dots attracted scientist’s attention because of their nontoxic, easy preparation and new optical properties. Iron nanoparticles were achieved with large-scale ball milling procedure. On the otherhand, cellulose acetate nano-fibers were prepared by electro spinning procedure (13KV, 20 cm for 1 h). Photoluminescence carbon dot were synthesized with citric acid and ethylene diamine applying hydrothermal method. Fe nanoparticles were added to polymer matrix and finally, carbon dots were coated on the surface of the fibers by hydrothermal method. Magnetic property and photo-luminescence are available simultaneously; addition of Hg2+ and Pb2+ ions to CDs solution leads to electron transfer and decreasing of PL. A novel magnetic nano-fibers as sensor for detecting of mercury(II) and lead(II) is presented. It is applicable for detection of dangerous heavy metals in places like industrial waste water, while because of magnetic filler can simply collected and did not have any probable danger due to nano dimension.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors are grateful to the council of Iran National Science Foundation; INSF (97017837) and University of Kashan for supporting this work by Grant No (159271/893330).

Journal of Materials Chemistry B, Materials for Biology and Medicine

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wang, L., Wu, B., Li, W., Li, Z., Zhan, J., Geng, B., Wang, S., Pan, D., & M. Wu. (2017). Industrial production of ultra-stable sulfonated graphene quantum dots for Golgi apparatus imaging, J. Mater. Chem. B. 5, 5355-5361

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Yu et al., 2018

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Multicolorful fluorescent-nanoprobe composed of Au nanocluster and carbon dots for colorimetric and fluorescent sensing Hg2+ and Cr6+
Sensors and Actuators B, Chemical

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Yu, L., Zhang, L., Ren, G., Li, S., Zhu, B., Chai, F., Qu, F., Wang, C., & Su, Z. (2018). Multicolorful fluorescent-nanoprobe composed of Au nanocluster and carbon dots for colorimetric and fluorescent sensing Hg2+ and Cr6+. Sensors and Actuators B: Chemical, 262, 678-686.

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zhu et al., 2009

Chemical Communications
